title: Febrile Child — Approach
oneLiner: Systematic assessment of a child with fever to identify serious bacterial infection (SBI) and guide safe management.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock:** cold peripheries, prolonged capillary refill time, tachycardia (despite fever), hypotension (late sign in children), altered consciousness. *Key finding: ill appearance + poor perfusion with respiratory distress — not just fever.*
- **Meningococcal disease / bacterial meningitis:** non-blanching purpuric rash (anywhere), neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, photophobia, Kernig’s sign. *Key finding: any non-blanching rash in a febrile child — press test with glass.*
- **At risk:** neonates (<3 months) especially <28 days + fever >38°C — treat as septic until proven otherwise.
sections.Presentation:
- Fever (subjective or measured >38°C) + one or more of:
  - Irritability/lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting, decreased wet nappies
  - Cough, tachypnoea, grunting, nasal flaring
  - Diarrhoea/vomiting
  - Rash (maculopapular, petechial, purpuric)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - General appearance (alert/irritable/lethargic) — use NICE traffic light system
  - Vital signs: HR, RR, BP (z-score charts), SpO₂, temperature, CRT
  - Capillary refill time (prolonged >2 seconds = shock)
  - Fontanelle (bulging = meningitis)
  - Neck stiffness (meningeal irritation)
  - Chest auscultation (crepitations, wheeze)
  - Ear exam (otitis media)
  - Throat/uvula (tonsillitis, epiglottitis)
  - Skin (rash, petechiae, purpura)
  - Abdomen (tenderness, guarding, masses)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Viral URTI** (coryza, no focal signs; well-looking)
  - **Otitis media** (ear pain, red bulging TM)
  - **Urinary tract infection** (fever without source, dysuria; urine dip + culture)
  - **Pneumonia** (cough, tachypnoea, focal chest signs, CXR)
  - **Bacteraemia/sepsis** (ill appearance, one or more vital sign derangements)
  - **Meningitis** (neck stiffness, photophobia, bulging fontanelle, non-blanching rash)
  - **Kawasaki disease** (unilateral cervical adenopathy, conjunctivitis, cracked lips, strawberry tongue, rash >5 days)
  - **Invasive group A streptococcal infection** (severe pain, rapidly progressive rash, hypotension)
- **Splitter:** Non-blanching rash + ill appearance → meningococcal sepsis / meningitis; no rash + well → viral URTI (but still consider UTI in infants).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia)
  - Urine dipstick (nitrites, leucocytes, protein — UTI screen)
  - Capillary refill test
  - Pulse oximetry
  - Quick neurological status (AVPU)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&E, LFTs, coagulation
  - Blood culture (prior to antibiotics — if antibiotics needed urgently, do not delay but prioritise culture)
  - Lactate (if shock suspected)
  - Blood gas (venous or arterial)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** if respiratory signs present (cough, tachypnoea, oxygen requirement)
  - **Abdominal ultrasound** if UTI not resolving or suspected obstruction
  - **CT head / MRI** if meningitis with focal neurology or raised ICP
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture** — only if no signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neurology, GCS <13, shock) AND after blood culture and before antibiotics (or within 1 hour of starting antibiotics). Contraindicated if coagulopathy or signs of meningococcal sepsis.
  - Viral swabs (nasopharyngeal / throat) if influenza/RSV suspected
  - Stool culture if diarrhoea with blood or persistent fever
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE traffic light system (fever in under 5s):** Red (immediate high risk), Amber (intermediate), Green (low risk). Decisions based on colour of skin, activity, respiratory status, circulation/hydration, other features.
  - **Sepsis criteria (qSOFA not validated in children; use NICE or PEWS)**
- **Never-miss test:** Blood culture before antibiotics in any child with fever and red/amber features (NICE). Omission leads to inability to tailor therapy and risk of missing fastidious organisms.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach:** airway, breathing (O₂ if SpO₂ <92%), circulation (IV/IO access, 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus if shocked, reassess; repeat up to 40 mL/kg)
  - **Antibiotics:** if red flags for sepsis/meningitis: IV cefotaxime OR ceftriaxone (see Drugs section). For neonates <3 months: IV cefotaxime + amoxicillin (per local policy). Give within 1 hour of decision.
  - **Antipyretics:** paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO or IV (if vomiting) for temperature >38.5°C + discomfort
  - **Treat dehydration:** oral rehydration salts (ORS) if mild-moderate; IV fluids if unable to tolerate or moderate-severe dehydration (0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose with K+ per local policy)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics according to culture results / clinical response
  - Monitor vital signs and PEWS (paediatric early warning score) q1-2h initially, reduce as stable
  - Fluid balance (strict input/output, urine output >1 mL/kg/h)
  - Repeat blood tests if no improvement (CRP trend, cultures)
  - Consider step-down to oral antibiotics if source identified and child improving (e.g., UTI, pneumonia)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course as directed (e.g., 7-10 days for UTI, 10 days for pneumonia)
  - Review in 48-72h if discharged with low-risk infection (e.g., viral URTI with amber features that resolved)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.45% NaCl + 5% dextrose (standard maintenance) but adjust for dehydration; per local policy (e.g., Holliday-Segar). If shock, use 0.9% NaCl boluses.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine in children unless specific risk factors (obesity, immobility, central line). Per local policy.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if risk of aspiration or meningism; otherwise encourage oral intake (breast milk/formula/cooled boiled water).
  - **Observation frequency:** PEWS q1h initially then q2-4h depending on stability.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol:** 15 mg/kg PO/IV every 4-6 hours (max 60 mg/kg/24h)
  - **Ibuprofen:** 5-10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours (max 30 mg/kg/24h); avoid in dehydrated child or suspected viraemia (risk of renal impairment)
  - **Cefotaxime:** 50 mg/kg IV every 6-8 hours (max 2 g per dose); for suspected sepsis/meningitis. Neonate <7 days: 50 mg/kg IV every 12h; 7-28 days: every 8h.
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 80 mg/kg IV every 24h (max 4 g/day); avoid in neonates (risk of bilirubin displacement). For older children >3 months.
  - **Cefotaxime + amoxicillin:** For neonates <3 months: cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV + amoxicillin [check local policy dose] (typically 50 mg/kg IV every 6h)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav (oral/IV) for UTI or pneumonia if sensitive
  - Clarithromycin if penicillin allergy (atypical coverage)
  - Gentamicin (7 mg/kg IV once daily) must check levels; avoid in renal impairment
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone — contraindicated in neonates (hyperbilirubinaemia risk) and with calcium-containing IV fluids
  - Ibuprofen — avoid in hypovolaemia, renal impairment, bleeding risk (NSAIDs)
  - Aminoglycosides — nephrotoxic and ototoxic; check levels
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin dose interval extended if eGFR <30; [check local policy]
  - Ceftriaxone adjusts in severe hepatic impairment; not typically needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving IV ceftriaxone to a jaundiced neonate without checking bilirubin — can cause kernicterus. Also, giving ibuprofen to a dehydrated child with fever — acute kidney injury.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock (hypotension, metabolic acidosis)
  - Meningitis (raised ICP, seizures, brain abscess)
  - Febrile convulsion (simple or complex; usually benign but rule out meningitis)
  - Dehydration (hypovolaemic shock)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in meningococcaemia
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological deficit post-meningitis (hearing loss, developmental delay)
  - Renal scarring following UTI (especially <1 year old)
  - Post-infectious issues (e.g., acute rheumatic fever, Guillain-Barré)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality from meningococcal disease ~5-10% with prompt treatment; higher if delayed. Bacterial meningitis <5% mortality in high-income settings, but up to 30% have long-term sequelae. Viral febrile illnesses almost always self-limiting.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always check a urine dipstick in any febrile child under 2 years — UTI is the most common bacterial infection.
- Use the NICE traffic light system as a cognitive checklist for red/amber/green features; document it.
- If performing LP, do it **after** blood culture and **before** antibiotics, but **do not delay antibiotics** more than 1 hour for LP. If LP delayed >1h, document reason.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do **not** give antibiotics to a well-looking child without a focus (risk of resistance, side effects); use NICE traffic light to guide.
- Do **not** use ibuprofen if there is any sign of dehydration (sunken eyes, prolonged CRT, dry mucous membranes) — NSAIDs worsen renal perfusion.
- Do **not** rely on temperature alone to assess severity — a very high fever with normal behaviour is usually benign; a low-grade fever with lethargy is a red flag.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening fever despite antipyretics or fever >5 days  
  - Lethargy, irritability, inconsolable crying  
  - New non-blanching rash, convulsion, difficulty breathing  
  - Not feeding/drinking for >6h (infants)  
  - Reduced wet nappies (<4 per day)
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If amber features but discharged: GP review within 24-48h  
  - If UTI confirmed: repeat urine culture after antibiotics; renal ultrasound if <6 months or recurrent UTI
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ST3+/Reg):**  
  - Any **red** feature on NICE traffic light (e.g., child looks pale/mottled/ashen, unresponsive, grunting, persistent fever >38°C in <3 months)  
  - PEWS score ≥4 (or NEWS2 if >12 years)  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 (any drop)  
  - Hypotension (< 5th centile for age)  
  - Shock not responding to 40 mL/kg fluid bolus  
- **Call ICU/retrieval team:**  
  - Respiratory failure (SpO₂ <90% despite O₂)  
  - Septic shock requiring inotropes (after second fluid bolus)  
  - Meningococcal purpura fulminans  
  - Concerns for raised ICP (Cushing’s triad, asymmetric pupils)

rawMarkdown:
# Febrile Child — Approach

**One-liner:** Systematic assessment of a child with fever to identify serious bacterial infection (SBI) and guide safe management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock:** cold peripheries, prolonged capillary refill time, tachycardia (despite fever), hypotension (late sign in children), altered consciousness. *Key finding: ill appearance + poor perfusion with respiratory distress — not just fever.*
- **Meningococcal disease / bacterial meningitis:** non-blanching purpuric rash (anywhere), neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, photophobia, Kernig’s sign. *Key finding: any non-blanching rash in a febrile child — press test with glass.*
- **At risk:** neonates (<3 months) especially <28 days + fever >38°C — treat as septic until proven otherwise.

### Presentation
- Fever (subjective or measured >38°C) + one or more of:
  - Irritability/lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting, decreased wet nappies
  - Cough, tachypnoea, grunting, nasal flaring
  - Diarrhoea/vomiting
  - Rash (maculopapular, petechial, purpuric)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - General appearance (alert/irritable/lethargic) — use NICE traffic light system
  - Vital signs: HR, RR, BP (z-score charts), SpO₂, temperature, CRT
  - Capillary refill time (prolonged >2 seconds = shock)
  - Fontanelle (bulging = meningitis)
  - Neck stiffness (meningeal irritation)
  - Chest auscultation (crepitations, wheeze)
  - Ear exam (otitis media)
  - Throat/uvula (tonsillitis, epiglottitis)
  - Skin (rash, petechiae, purpura)
  - Abdomen (tenderness, guarding, masses)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Viral URTI** (coryza, no focal signs; well-looking)
  - **Otitis media** (ear pain, red bulging TM)
  - **Urinary tract infection** (fever without source, dysuria; urine dip + culture)
  - **Pneumonia** (cough, tachypnoea, focal chest signs, CXR)
  - **Bacteraemia/sepsis** (ill appearance, one or more vital sign derangements)
  - **Meningitis** (neck stiffness, photophobia, bulging fontanelle, non-blanching rash)
  - **Kawasaki disease** (unilateral cervical adenopathy, conjunctivitis, cracked lips, strawberry tongue, rash >5 days)
  - **Invasive group A streptococcal infection** (severe pain, rapidly progressive rash, hypotension)
- **Splitter:** Non-blanching rash + ill appearance → meningococcal sepsis / meningitis; no rash + well → viral URTI (but still consider UTI in infants).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia)
  - Urine dipstick (nitrites, leucocytes, protein — UTI screen)
  - Capillary refill test
  - Pulse oximetry
  - Quick neurological status (AVPU)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&E, LFTs, coagulation
  - Blood culture (prior to antibiotics — if antibiotics needed urgently, do not delay but prioritise culture)
  - Lactate (if shock suspected)
  - Blood gas (venous or arterial)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** if respiratory signs present (cough, tachypnoea, oxygen requirement)
  - **Abdominal ultrasound** if UTI not resolving or suspected obstruction
  - **CT head / MRI** if meningitis with focal neurology or raised ICP
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture** — only if no signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neurology, GCS <13, shock) AND after blood culture and before antibiotics (or within 1 hour of starting antibiotics). Contraindicated if coagulopathy or signs of meningococcal sepsis.
  - Viral swabs (nasopharyngeal / throat) if influenza/RSV suspected
  - Stool culture if diarrhoea with blood or persistent fever
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE traffic light system (fever in under 5s):** Red (immediate high risk), Amber (intermediate), Green (low risk). Decisions based on colour of skin, activity, respiratory status, circulation/hydration, other features.
  - **Sepsis criteria (qSOFA not validated in children; use NICE or PEWS)**
- **Never-miss test:** Blood culture before antibiotics in any child with fever and red/amber features (NICE). Omission leads to inability to tailor therapy and risk of missing fastidious organisms.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach:** airway, breathing (O₂ if SpO₂ <92%), circulation (IV/IO access, 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus if shocked, reassess; repeat up to 40 mL/kg)
  - **Antibiotics:** if red flags for sepsis/meningitis: IV cefotaxime OR ceftriaxone (see Drugs section). For neonates <3 months: IV cefotaxime + amoxicillin (per local policy). Give within 1 hour of decision.
  - **Antipyretics:** paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO or IV (if vomiting) for temperature >38.5°C + discomfort
  - **Treat dehydration:** oral rehydration salts (ORS) if mild-moderate; IV fluids if unable to tolerate or moderate-severe dehydration (0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose with K+ per local policy)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics according to culture results / clinical response
  - Monitor vital signs and PEWS (paediatric early warning score) q1-2h initially, reduce as stable
  - Fluid balance (strict input/output, urine output >1 mL/kg/h)
  - Repeat blood tests if no improvement (CRP trend, cultures)
  - Consider step-down to oral antibiotics if source identified and child improving (e.g., UTI, pneumonia)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course as directed (e.g., 7-10 days for UTI, 10 days for pneumonia)
  - Review in 48-72h if discharged with low-risk infection (e.g., viral URTI with amber features that resolved)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.45% NaCl + 5% dextrose (standard maintenance) but adjust for dehydration; per local policy (e.g., Holliday-Segar). If shock, use 0.9% NaCl boluses.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine in children unless specific risk factors (obesity, immobility, central line). Per local policy.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if risk of aspiration or meningism; otherwise encourage oral intake (breast milk/formula/cooled boiled water).
  - **Observation frequency:** PEWS q1h initially then q2-4h depending on stability.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol:** 15 mg/kg PO/IV every 4-6 hours (max 60 mg/kg/24h)
  - **Ibuprofen:** 5-10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours (max 30 mg/kg/24h); avoid in dehydrated child or suspected viraemia (risk of renal impairment)
  - **Cefotaxime:** 50 mg/kg IV every 6-8 hours (max 2 g per dose); for suspected sepsis/meningitis. Neonate <7 days: 50 mg/kg IV every 12h; 7-28 days: every 8h.
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 80 mg/kg IV every 24h (max 4 g/day); avoid in neonates (risk of bilirubin displacement). For older children >3 months.
  - **Cefotaxime + amoxicillin:** For neonates <3 months: cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV + amoxicillin [check local policy dose] (typically 50 mg/kg IV every 6h)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav (oral/IV) for UTI or pneumonia if sensitive
  - Clarithromycin if penicillin allergy (atypical coverage)
  - Gentamicin (7 mg/kg IV once daily) must check levels; avoid in renal impairment
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone — contraindicated in neonates (hyperbilirubinaemia risk) and with calcium-containing IV fluids
  - Ibuprofen — avoid in hypovolaemia, renal impairment, bleeding risk (NSAIDs)
  - Aminoglycosides — nephrotoxic and ototoxic; check levels
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin dose interval extended if eGFR <30; [check local policy]
  - Ceftriaxone adjusts in severe hepatic impairment; not typically needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving IV ceftriaxone to a jaundiced neonate without checking bilirubin — can cause kernicterus. Also, giving ibuprofen to a dehydrated child with fever — acute kidney injury.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock (hypotension, metabolic acidosis)
  - Meningitis (raised ICP, seizures, brain abscess)
  - Febrile convulsion (simple or complex; usually benign but rule out meningitis)
  - Dehydration (hypovolaemic shock)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in meningococcaemia
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological deficit post-meningitis (hearing loss, developmental delay)
  - Renal scarring following UTI (especially <1 year old)
  - Post-infectious issues (e.g., acute rheumatic fever, Guillain-Barré)

### Prognosis
- Mortality from meningococcal disease ~5-10% with prompt treatment; higher if delayed. Bacterial meningitis <5% mortality in high-income settings, but up to 30% have long-term sequelae. Viral febrile illnesses almost always self-limiting.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always check a urine dipstick in any febrile child under 2 years — UTI is the most common bacterial infection.
- Use the NICE traffic light system as a cognitive checklist for red/amber/green features; document it.
- If performing LP, do it **after** blood culture and **before** antibiotics, but **do not delay antibiotics** more than 1 hour for LP. If LP delayed >1h, document reason.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do **not** give antibiotics to a well-looking child without a focus (risk of resistance, side effects); use NICE traffic light to guide.
- Do **not** use ibuprofen if there is any sign of dehydration (sunken eyes, prolonged CRT, dry mucous membranes) — NSAIDs worsen renal perfusion.
- Do **not** rely on temperature alone to assess severity — a very high fever with normal behaviour is usually benign; a low-grade fever with lethargy is a red flag.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening fever despite antipyretics or fever >5 days  
  - Lethargy, irritability, inconsolable crying  
  - New non-blanching rash, convulsion, difficulty breathing  
  - Not feeding/drinking for >6h (infants)  
  - Reduced wet nappies (<4 per day)
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If amber features but discharged: GP review within 24-48h  
  - If UTI confirmed: repeat urine culture after antibiotics; renal ultrasound if <6 months or recurrent UTI  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ST3+/Reg):**  
  - Any **red** feature on NICE traffic light (e.g., child looks pale/mottled/ashen, unresponsive, grunting, persistent fever >38°C in <3 months)  
  - PEWS score ≥4 (or NEWS2 if >12 years)  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 (any drop)  
  - Hypotension (< 5th centile for age)  
  - Shock not responding to 40 mL/kg fluid bolus  
- **Call ICU/retrieval team:**  
  - Respiratory failure (SpO₂ <90% despite O₂)  
  - Septic shock requiring inotropes (after second fluid bolus)  
  - Meningococcal purpura fulminans  
  - Concerns for raised ICP (Cushing’s triad, asymmetric pupils)
